title: My brain is changing, not failing
subtitle: An affirmation for perimenopause brain fog
excerpt: For the word-finding pauses, the walked-into-a-room-and-forgotten-why moments, and the slow sentences that feel like evidence of something going wrong. It isn't going wrong. It's changing.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. Framing drawn from public-domain perimenopause literature on cognitive symptoms during hormonal transition.

practice.whenToUse: When brain fog, word-finding difficulty, or a sense of cognitive slippage triggers the fear that something is seriously wrong

content.text: Perimenopause brain fog has a specific character: the word that's gone, the sentence that slows, the calculation that used to be instant. It arrives in the middle of a competent life and feels like evidence that something is going wrong. It isn't. The cognitive changes of perimenopause are well-documented, common, and, for most women, temporary.
Say this once, slow, out loud or in your mind. Then sit with it for a moment before you carry on.
My brain is changing, not failing.
If one repetition feels thin, say it three times.
By homemade.education. Research shows these changes are hormonal, not structural. They improve after menopause for most women.
